concepts.name: Polymers – Definition and Terminology
concepts.explanation: Polymers are high molecular mass macromolecules (10³-10⁷ u) formed by the joining of repeating structural units called monomers. The process of formation is called polymerisation. Polymers can be natural, semi-synthetic, or synthetic.

concepts.name: Classification of Polymers – Based on Source
concepts.explanation: Natural polymers are found in plants and animals (e.g., proteins, cellulose, starch, natural rubber). Semi-synthetic polymers are chemically modified natural polymers (e.g., cellulose acetate, cellulose nitrate). Synthetic polymers are man-made (e.g., polythene, nylon 6,6, Buna-S).

concepts.name: Addition Polymerisation (Chain Growth Polymerisation)
concepts.explanation: Addition polymerisation involves the repeated addition of monomer molecules (usually unsaturated compounds like alkenes, dienes) without the loss of any small molecule. It proceeds through free radical, cationic, or anionic mechanisms.

concepts.name: Mechanism of Addition Polymerisation – Free Radical Mechanism
concepts.explanation: The free radical mechanism involves three steps: (1) Chain initiation – formation of a free radical from an initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide); (2) Chain propagation – the radical adds to monomer, generating a larger radical; (3) Chain termination – two radicals combine to stop chain growth.

concepts.name: Homopolymers and Copolymers
concepts.explanation: Homopolymers are formed from a single monomeric species (e.g., polythene from ethene). Copolymers are formed from two or more different monomers (e.g., Buna-S from 1,3-butadiene and styrene).

concepts.name: Low Density Polythene (LDP)
concepts.explanation: LDP is obtained by polymerisation of ethene at high pressure (1000-2000 atm) and temperature (350-570 K) in the presence of a peroxide initiator. It has a highly branched structure, is chemically inert, tough, flexible, and a poor conductor of electricity. Uses: insulation, squeeze bottles, toys, flexible pipes.

concepts.name: High Density Polythene (HDP)
concepts.explanation: HDP is obtained by polymerisation of ethene in a hydrocarbon solvent with Ziegler-Natta catalyst (triethylaluminium + titanium tetrachloride) at 333-343 K and 6-7 atm. It has linear molecules, is more tough and hard than LDP. Uses: buckets, dustbins, bottles, pipes.

concepts.name: Polytetrafluoroethene (Teflon)
concepts.explanation: Teflon is obtained by heating tetrafluoroethene with a free radical or persulphate catalyst at high pressure. It is chemically inert and resistant to corrosive reagents. Uses: oil seals, gaskets, non-stick surfaces for utensils.

concepts.name: Polyacrylonitrile (Orlon, Acrilan)
concepts.explanation: Polyacrylonitrile is obtained by the addition polymerisation of acrylonitrile (CH₂=CHCN) in the presence of a peroxide catalyst. It is used as a substitute for wool in making commercial fibres (orlon, acrilan).

concepts.name: Condensation Polymerisation (Step Growth Polymerisation)
concepts.explanation: Condensation polymerisation involves the repetitive condensation reaction between bi-functional or tri-functional monomers, with the loss of small molecules (H₂O, HCl, alcohol, etc.). Each step produces a distinct functionalised species. Examples: polyamides, polyesters.

concepts.name: Nylon 6,6 (Polyamide)
concepts.explanation: Nylon 6,6 is prepared by the condensation polymerisation of hexamethylenediamine (H₂N-(CH₂)₆-NH₂) with adipic acid (HOOC-(CH₂)₄-COOH) under high pressure and temperature. It has high tensile strength due to strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding. Uses: sheets, bristles, textiles.

concepts.name: Nylon 6
concepts.explanation: Nylon 6 is obtained by heating caprolactam with water at high temperature. Caprolactam is a cyclic amide that undergoes ring-opening polymerisation. Uses: tyre cords, fabrics, ropes.

concepts.name: Polyesters – Terylene (Dacron)
concepts.explanation: Terylene (Dacron) is prepared by the condensation polymerisation of ethylene glycol (HO-CH₂-CH₂-OH) with terephthalic acid (p-HOOC-C₆H₄-COOH) at 420-460 K in the presence of zinc acetate-antimony trioxide catalyst. It is crease-resistant and used in blending with cotton and wool.

concepts.name: Phenol-Formaldehyde Polymers – Bakelite
concepts.explanation: Phenol-formaldehyde polymers are obtained by condensation of phenol with formaldehyde in the presence of acid or base. The initial product, novolac (linear), on heating with formaldehyde undergoes cross-linking to form bakelite (thermosetting). Uses: combs, electrical switches, handles of utensils.

concepts.name: Thermoplastics and Thermosetting Polymers
concepts.explanation: Thermoplastics soften on heating and harden on cooling (reversible). Examples: polythene, PVC, nylon. Thermosetting polymers become infusible on heating and cannot be remoulded (irreversible). Examples: bakelite, urea-formaldehyde resin.

concepts.name: Copolymerisation
concepts.explanation: Copolymerisation is the polymerisation of a mixture of more than one monomeric species to form a copolymer. Copolymers have properties different from homopolymers. Example: Buna-S (1,3-butadiene + styrene) is tough and a good substitute for natural rubber.

concepts.name: Natural Rubber – Structure and Properties
concepts.explanation: Natural rubber is a linear polymer of isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene) with cis-1,4-polyisoprene structure. Chains are held by weak van der Waals forces, giving elastic properties. It softens at high temperature, becomes brittle at low temperature, and is soluble in non-polar solvents.

concepts.name: Vulcanisation of Rubber
concepts.explanation: Vulcanisation improves the physical properties of natural rubber by heating it with sulphur (5% for tyre rubber) and additives at 373-415 K. Sulphur forms cross-links at the double bonds, stiffening the rubber and making it more resistant to temperature, solvents, and oxidation.

concepts.name: Neoprene (Polychloroprene)
concepts.explanation: Neoprene is a synthetic rubber formed by the free radical polymerisation of chloroprene (2-chloro-1,3-butadiene). It has superior resistance to vegetable and mineral oils. Uses: conveyor belts, gaskets, hoses.

concepts.name: Buna-N and Buna-S
concepts.explanation: Buna-N is a copolymer of 1,3-butadiene and acrylonitrile (resistant to petrol, oil). Buna-S is a copolymer of 1,3-butadiene and styrene (used for autotypes, floor tiles). Both are synthetic rubbers prepared by addition polymerisation.

concepts.name: Biodegradable Polymers
concepts.explanation: Biodegradable polymers are designed to degrade in the environment. They contain functional groups similar to biopolymers. Examples: PHBV (poly-β-hydroxybutyrate-co-β-hydroxyvalerate) – used in packaging, orthopaedic devices; Nylon-2-nylon-6 – alternating polyamide of glycine and amino caproic acid.

concepts.name: Polypropylene (PP)
concepts.explanation: Polypropylene is obtained by the addition polymerisation of propene. It is used for manufacturing ropes, toys, pipes, and fibres.

concepts.name: Polystyrene (PS)
concepts.explanation: Polystyrene is obtained by the addition polymerisation of styrene. It is used as an insulator, wrapping material, and for manufacturing toys and radio/TV cabinets.

concepts.name: Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)
concepts.explanation: PVC is obtained by the addition polymerisation of vinyl chloride (CH₂=CHCl). It is used for manufacturing raincoats, handbags, vinyl flooring, and water pipes.

concepts.name: Glyptal (Alkyd Resin)
concepts.explanation: Glyptal is a polyester formed by the condensation polymerisation of ethylene glycol and phthalic acid. It is used in the manufacture of paints and lacquers.

concepts.name: Urea-Formaldehyde Resin
concepts.explanation: Urea-formaldehyde resin is a condensation polymer of urea and formaldehyde. It is used for making unbreakable cups and laminated sheets.
